title: Loss Contingencies — Accrue, Disclose, or Ignore
event: Hollis Co. faces a lawsuit its attorneys call PROBABLE to lose, with damages reasonably estimated between $500,000 and $700,000 and no best estimate within the range. Compute the amount accrued under U.S. GAAP.

computationPaths.narration: GAAP's grid: probable + estimable → accrue; reasonably possible → disclose; remote → nothing. Within a range with no better point, GAAP accrues the MINIMUM and discloses the rest.

memorize.body: Probable + estimable = ACCRUE. Reasonably possible = DISCLOSE. Remote = SILENCE. Range with no best point: GAAP takes the minimum (IFRS takes the midpoint).
GAIN contingencies are never accrued — conservatism runs one direction. Disclose cautiously at most.

questions.prompt: Probable loss, range $500,000–$700,000, no best estimate. GAAP accrual = ?

distractors.feedback: The midpoint is the IFRS answer. U.S. GAAP accrues the MINIMUM of the range: 500,000.
The maximum is disclosed, not accrued. GAAP books the low end: 500,000.
Probable AND estimable means accrual is REQUIRED — disclosure alone is the reasonably-possible treatment.
